The contrast phenomenon states that two things that are different from one another will be perceived as even more different if they are presented one right after the other. This shows how the context established before making a request often can be more important than the way the request itself is made. One business example provides a good illustration. A manufacturer of hot tubs came out with a deluxe version of their Whirlpool Spa. Their standard approach to selling to potential buyers was to show the cheapest spa first, then the moderately priced spa, and finally the deluxe spa model. Very few people even considered buying the deluxe spa at first because of the price. Most customers said that compared to the spa's price from $10,000 to $15,000, the $30,000 luxury spa just seemed to cost too much. After being called in to address this problem, my team of graduate students and I visited the San Diego Spa Company and decided to interview the people who had actually bought the luxury $30,000 spa. There were a few of them. Those customers who had purchased the high-end model said they absolutely loved it. The spa featured extra cup holders, additional high-powered jets, room for eight people, colored lights, a deck, a sound system, and a gazebo. Several of the luxury spa buyers said, now our friends come over for the spa. Our kids stay home for the spa, and we often have family get-togethers around it. It was like adding another room to the house. My team and I reviewed those comments and asked the store manager to instruct their sales people to use the idea of contrast by bringing potential buyers over to the deluxe model first. I'm honestly telling them that many people who have purchased this model say, we love it. It was like adding another room to the house. Then we instructed the salesperson to ask the potential buyer, how much do you suppose adding a room to your house would cost? Salespeople would often say at least $80,000 to $100,000. Well, the salespeople would then say, this spa is only $30,000. This honest and subtle change created a contrast between the cost of a whole new room with the lesser expense of adding a luxury spa, which some people said was just as good as an addition to the house. How much do you think sales of the deluxe spa then increased after salespeople started using this honest contrast approach? 5%? 10%? 15%? 25%? No. Sales increased by 520%. That's right. Ethically using the contrast phenomenon provided a sales increase of 520%. Now notice that the use of a contrast was most successful when the salespeople mentioned the price of a room addition before the price of the spa, not after. It's crucial that we do our homework to identify what legitimate contrasts are genuinely present and available so that we can bring them to the attention of our prospects. By taking the time to do our homework in this regard, we are able to raise meaningful contrast to the surface of our prospects' minds by posing to them the question, compared to what?
